categoryDescription: Looks at the slow-leak factors that quietly decide whether long-term returns reach the investor or get eaten up by costs: fees, what you actually earn for those fees, the recurring spread cost of trading, the people running the fund, and tax drag. Five factors apply to every ETF group. `factorDescription` carries the generic measurement principle and Pass/Fail rule. `groupInstructions` carries the group-specific perspective. When the two seem to conflict, follow `groupInstructions`.

factors.factorDescription: Compares the fund's yearly fee to other funds in the same and similar categories. The cheapest peer that gives the same exposure is the honest reference, and similar sibling categories inside the same ETF group are fair comparison too. A higher fee is fine only when the fund earns it — through a real edge the cheaper peer cannot give. Pass when the fee is at or below the category median. Fail when the fee is well above peers without a clear reason.

groupInstructions.broad-equity: The strictest fee bar of any group — passive broad-equity peers run very cheap. The cheapest passive sibling on the same exposure sets the reference point. Anything materially above the median needs a real reason. Verdict band: Strong = fee at or near the cheapest passive sibling; In Line = within ±10% of category median; Weak / Fail = ≥10% above category median without offsetting value-add.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Compare to the broadest passive sector or theme peer on the same exposure. Thematic funds are usually pricier than plain sector ETFs — that is only fine if the fund earns it. Verdict band: Strong = fee ≥10% below category median; In Line = within ±10%; Weak / Fail = ≥10% above category median without offsetting value-add.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Compare to other funds at the same leverage on the same underlying. The headline fee is only one slice of the cost — financing and daily-reset drag sit on top. Verdict band: Strong = fee at or below the leverage-bucket median; In Line = within ±10%; Weak / Fail = ≥10% above peer median without offsetting daily-tracking quality.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Compare inside the same wrapper type — physical to physical, futures to futures, spot crypto to spot crypto. Wrappers carry different cost stacks, so cross-wrapper comparisons mislead. Verdict band: Strong = fee ≥10% below the wrapper-peer median; In Line = within ±10%; Weak / Fail = ≥10% above wrapper-peer median without offsetting structural advantage.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Compare to other option-income funds on the same underlying. The fee has to be paid for by yield, downside cushion, or smoother total return. Verdict band: Strong = fee ≥10% below option-income peer median; In Line = within ±10%; Weak / Fail = ≥10% above peer median without offsetting yield or downside protection.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Compare to peers with the same equity / bond mix or the same target year. A simple DIY mix of cheap index funds is the honest alternative for retail. Verdict band: Strong = fee ≥10% below allocation-peer median; In Line = within ±10%; Weak / Fail = ≥10% above peer median without offsetting tactical or glide-path edge.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Strict bar — passive IG bond and Treasury peers are very cheap. Active or muni funds need a real edge to justify any meaningful fee gap. Verdict band: Strong = fee at or near the cheapest passive sibling; In Line = within ±10% of category median; Weak / Fail = ≥10% above category median without offsetting active alpha or muni-specific value.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Compare to peers in the same credit tier and sub-asset (high-yield, bank loan, preferred, EM debt, convertible). Active credit funds need documented value-add after fees. Verdict band: Strong = fee ≥10% below credit-tier peer median; In Line = within ±10%; Weak / Fail = ≥10% above peer median without offsetting credit-research alpha.

factors.factorDescription: Asks the honest question: if you pay more, do you actually get more? A higher fee is fine when net returns after fees beat the cheaper alternative over multi-year windows. A higher fee on similar or weaker returns is just a drag. Pass when an above-peer fee is matched by above-peer net returns, or the fee is already in line with the cheapest passive option. Fail when the fee is high but net returns trail cheaper peers.
factors.factorMetrics: expenseRatio, overviewAdjExpenseRatio, return3y, return5y, returnsTrailing, returnsAnnual, overviewCategory

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Compare net total return over 5Y / 10Y to the cheapest passive sibling. A fee gap should show up as a return gap — otherwise the higher fee is pure drag on the same exposure. Verdict band: Strong = net return ≥2 pp above the cheap peer; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp below the cheap peer.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Compare net total return to the cheapest broad sector ETF in the same space. If a pricier thematic fund just tracks the broad sector after fees, retail is better off with the cheap peer. Verdict band: Strong = net return ≥2 pp above the cheap sector peer; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp below the cheap peer.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Compare daily-tracking fidelity and realized return per unit of underlying move to peers in the same leverage bucket. Decay is structural — excess decay vs peers is a fee problem. Verdict band: Strong = realized result clearly above the leverage-bucket median; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp below the bucket median.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Compare tracking gap vs spot (or vs the underlying index) to peers in the same wrapper. A wider gap than the fee is drag the cheaper peer probably doesn't have. Verdict band: Strong = net tracking gap ≥2 pp tighter than wrapper-peer median; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp wider gap than wrapper-peer median.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Compare total return (price + distributions) to a cheap high-dividend ETF plus a simple covered-call overlay. If the fund doesn't beat that, the fee isn't earned. Verdict band: Strong = total return ≥2 pp above the cheap blended benchmark; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp below.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Compare net return to a simple cheap blend (e.g. broad equity + core bond at near-zero fee) over multi-year windows. The value-add is automatic rebalancing — if the fund doesn't beat the DIY mix, the extra fee is hard to justify. Verdict band: Strong = net return ≥2 pp above the cheap DIY blend; In Line = within ±2 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥2 pp below.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Compare net total return to a cheap passive sibling of the same duration. A fee gap should be paid for by yield or active alpha — otherwise the cheap passive option wins. Verdict band uses narrow thresholds for bonds: Strong = net return ≥0.5 pp above the cheap passive sibling; In Line = within ±0.5 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥0.5 pp below.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Compare net total return to a cheap passive credit sibling of the same tier. Active credit should show documented manager alpha after fees — otherwise the cheap passive option wins. Verdict band uses narrow thresholds for credit: Strong = net return ≥0.5 pp above the cheap passive sibling; In Line = within ±0.5 pp; Weak / Fail = ≥0.5 pp below.

factors.factorDescription: Measures the cost retail pays every time they enter or exit the fund — a recurring drag that sits outside the expense ratio and compounds with every rebalance, dividend reinvestment, or dollar-cost-averaging contribution. The 30-day median bid-ask spread (in basis points) is the headline measure. A 2 bps spread on a fund traded once a year is trivial; a persistent 30 bps spread on a fund a retail investor DCAs into monthly costs more than the expense ratio every year. Spread quality is downstream of underlying liquidity, AUM supporting tight market-maker quoting, and authorized-participant arbitrage health — but treated here purely as the recurring cost retail pays to transact, not as a stress-event risk. Pass when the median spread is at or below category norm for the fund's structure. Fail when the spread is persistently wide vs peers in normal market conditions, making the fund materially more expensive to own than the expense ratio suggests.

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Tightest bar — mega-cap passive ETFs (VOO, VTI, IVV, SPY) trade at 1-2 bps. Anything above 5 bps on a plain US large-cap tracker signals thin AP support or low volume. Small-cap and international broad trackers run 3-10 bps as normal.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: S&P sector ETFs (XL- series, VGT) trade at 1-3 bps. Thematic and niche ETFs commonly run 10-40 bps in normal conditions — material vs the headline fee for a retail investor making monthly contributions.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: TQQQ, SOXL, UPRO trade at 1-3 bps because of massive volume. Smaller leveraged products can run 10-30 bps even in calm markets — a critical cost because the use case is rapid trading with multiple round-trips.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: GLD, IAU, SLV: 1-3 bps. Spot Bitcoin ETFs (IBIT, FBTC): 2-5 bps. Futures-based commodity funds: 5-20 bps. Smaller single-commodity wrappers can hit 30-100 bps in normal conditions — material vs the headline fee.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: JEPI, JEPQ: 2-4 bps. Smaller covered-call and defined-outcome ETFs run 10-40 bps. The spread is especially consequential because these funds attract income-seekers who reinvest distributions monthly.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Vanguard / BlackRock / iShares allocation ETFs: 2-5 bps. Tactical allocation ETFs can run 15-40 bps. Matters most for retail using these as DCA buy-and-hold vehicles.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: AGG, BND, VGIT: 1-3 bps. Long-duration Treasury (TLT) similar. Muni ETFs (MUB, VTEB) at 2-5 bps. Single-state muni ETFs run wider (10-30 bps) — flag for retail rebalancing in taxable accounts.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: HYG, JNK, LQD: 2-5 bps in normal conditions. EM debt (EMB) and bank-loan (BKLN): 5-15 bps. Preferred ETFs (PFF): 3-10 bps. Anything materially above these bands is a cost flag.

factors.factorTitle: Issuer Quality, Manager Tenure & Track Record
factors.factorDescription: Looks at four things together: (1) issuer reputation and operational scale — large, established ETF issuers run tight, well-supervised operations; smaller or newer issuers carry real operational risk. (2) Manager tenure and continuity — turnover within the past 2-3 years on an active or strategy fund is a yellow flag. (3) Fund age and operational history — 10+ years means multiple market cycles evaluated; 5-10Y is decent signal; 3-5Y partial; under 3Y is effectively 'new' and must lean on issuer credibility and strategy simplicity. (4) Mandate stability — quietly changing benchmark, strategy, or category breaks the usability of the historical record and is a real red flag. The specific established-issuer set and the importance of named managers vary by group and is named in `groupInstructions`. AUM trajectory context is referenced here only as background — the live AUM-trajectory call lives in the past-returns `aum_growth_trend` factor. Pass when the issuer is established AND the fund has 5+ years of stable mandate AND manager continuity is at least 3-5 years, OR the fund is younger than 5Y but comes from an established issuer running a proven strategy. Fail when a niche or newer issuer is running a complex active strategy with thin operational history, OR documented recent strategy / benchmark / category changes, OR active-fund manager churn without a clear succession story. Do NOT Fail on age alone — anchor on issuer credibility and strategy simplicity when track record is short.
factors.factorMetrics: issuer, numberOfManagers, longestTenure, averageTenure, advisors, currentManagers, inceptionDate, strategyText, overviewCategory

groupInstructions.broad-equity: Issuer reputation dominates — for passive index trackers, named managers are largely symbolic. Mega-issuers (Vanguard, BlackRock, State Street, Schwab, Fidelity, Invesco) are the safe choices. Smaller issuers running a passive broad-equity fund are acceptable when AUM and tracking quality are healthy. Don't flag a 33-year manager tenure on a 33-year-old fund as a strength — that's just fund age, not a comparative signal.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: For passive sector / thematic funds, issuer matters most. For actively-managed thematic ETFs, manager continuity is decisive — flag any churn. Theme reclassifications (a 'robotics' fund quietly becoming an 'AI' fund) are real mandate changes.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Issuer operational track record on leveraged products is the anchor — the major leveraged-product issuers dominate the space. Smaller issuers running 3x products without a track record carry real operational risk.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Issuer matters more here than for plain equity because of custody, audit, and structural integrity. Major issuers carry the operational scale; smaller issuers running spot-commodity or crypto wrappers need scrutiny on custody arrangements and audit cadence.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Manager continuity is decisive for these strategy-driven funds. Newer issuers running complex options strategies with thin track records carry real execution risk.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Glide-path architect AND active-sleeve manager continuity both matter. For tactical / global allocation, individual decision-makers drive outcomes — flag any manager change.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: For passive IG / Treasury funds, issuer dominates. For active IG core / core-plus, manager continuity matters. For single-state muni, newer issuers are acceptable when issuer / advisor research depth is documented.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: Active credit demands manager expertise — HY / bank-loan / EM-debt funds rely on credit-research teams. For passive HY / preferred ETFs, issuer scale is sufficient.

factors.factorDescription: Measures how much of the fund's return is lost to tax drag in a taxable brokerage account, and what tax character its distributions have. ETFs are structurally tax-efficient — in-kind creation / redemption keeps capital-gain distributions rare — but not all ETFs are equal. Three dimensions matter: (1) capital-gain distribution history (a flag for active or thematic equity ETFs and for daily-leveraged products); (2) distribution composition — qualified dividends taxed favorably (max 23.8% federal long-term) vs ordinary income (marginal rate up to 37%+) vs short-term gain (marginal) vs return-of-capital (ROC is tax-deferred but lowers cost basis, not a free lunch); (3) structural quirks — K-1 reporting for partnership-structured commodity funds adds tax-time friction; physically-backed precious metals are taxed at the collectibles rate (max 28%); muni-bond income is federal-tax-exempt (and state-tax-exempt for in-state holders); daily-leveraged products generate frequent cap-gain distributions from the swap-reset mechanism. For tax-deferred accounts (IRA, 401(k)) this factor is less decisive — but most retail dollars sit in taxable accounts, so it still matters. Pass when the fund has no meaningful recent cap-gain distribution history (passive equity) OR distribution character is well-disclosed and reasonable for the strategy. Fail when there are material cap-gain distributions despite a passive structure, OR high-yield income is largely ROC / short-term gain without clear disclosure, OR the structure creates unexpected tax burden (K-1) without offsetting benefit.
factors.factorMetrics: overviewTurnover, reportedTurnoverPct, dividendYield, overviewSecYield, overviewTtmYield, overviewCategory, strategyText

groupInstructions.broad-equity: The ETF structure makes broad-equity index funds very tax-efficient. The mechanism: in-kind creation / redemption flushes out embedded gains, so most passive trackers essentially never pay capital-gain distributions, and most of the income is qualified dividends taxed at the long-term cap-gains rate (max 23.8% federal). What to check for a Pass: no meaningful capital-gain distributions in the last 3-5 years AND most distributions are qualified dividends. What triggers a Fail: actively-managed broad-equity ETFs that paid capital-gain distributions in recent years (real friction for retail in taxable accounts), OR a meaningful share of distributions is ordinary income (e.g. REIT-heavy or MLP-heavy funds) when the marketing label suggests plain equity dividends, OR the fund is in a mutual-fund wrapper that lacks ETF in-kind tax efficiency.
groupInstructions.sector-thematic-equity: Most passive sector and thematic ETFs are tax-efficient because in-kind redemption removes embedded gains. What to check for a Pass: no recent capital-gain distributions AND distribution character matches what the marketing label suggests. What triggers a Fail: (1) actively-managed thematic ETFs with high turnover that have paid capital-gain distributions in the last 3 years; (2) REIT-focused funds (real-estate sectors) — distributions are non-qualified dividends taxed at marginal rates, not long-term cap-gains rates; flag explicitly even if no cap-gain distros; (3) MLP-focused funds (some energy infrastructure) — generate K-1 forms and have UBTI considerations even in IRAs; flag explicitly. Plain passive sector ETFs without these issues are a Pass.
groupInstructions.leveraged-inverse: Materially tax-inefficient — daily swap-reset generates frequent capital-gain distributions, often taxed as short-term gains at marginal rates. Best held in a tax-advantaged account, but these are traded short-term anyway so the tax friction hits every realized trade. State this explicitly for retail.
groupInstructions.commodities-and-digital-assets: Structure changes everything. (1) Physically-backed precious metals → collectibles rate (max 28% in taxable accounts, higher than long-term cap-gains 15-20%). (2) Futures-based commodity funds → often K-1 partnership reporting; mark-to-market 60/40 tax on Section 1256 contracts (60% long-term, 40% short-term regardless of holding period). (3) Spot crypto wrappers → 1099 grantor-trust pass-through; relatively simple tax. Name the structure and its tax implication explicitly.
groupInstructions.derivative-income: Distribution composition is the central tax question. Some option-income funds distribute qualified dividends plus ELN income taxed as ordinary income. Others carry a large ROC share (tax-deferred but lowers cost basis). Headline yield alone misleads — quote the ROC share and frame the after-tax effective yield at a typical retail bracket. Best held in IRA / 401(k); flag for retail in taxable accounts.
groupInstructions.allocation-target-date: Generally tax-efficient — qualified dividends + interest-income mix matches the underlying sleeves. The bond-sleeve portion generates ordinary interest income, so allocation funds with heavy bond sleeves (conservative, target-date nearing retirement at 40-60% bonds) are less tax-efficient than pure equity. Better held in tax-advantaged accounts for the bond-heavy end of the glide path.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-investment-grade: Treasury and agency interest is federal-only taxable (state-tax-exempt). Muni interest is federal-tax-exempt (and state-tax-exempt for in-state holders) — quote tax-equivalent yield at a representative bracket (~32% federal, plus state for in-state funds) so retail can compare to taxable bond peers. TIPS funds generate phantom income (inflation-adjusted principal taxable annually) — best held in IRA.
groupInstructions.fixed-income-credit-and-income: HY / bank-loan / EM-debt / multisector distributions are ordinary interest income, taxed at marginal rates — less tax-efficient than qualified-dividend equity. Best held in IRA / 401(k). Preferred-stock ETFs are a mix of qualified and ordinary distributions — less tax-efficient than common-stock dividends. EM debt may carry foreign withholding taxes.
